data.title: Xtract One Technologies Inc. 2025 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Xtract One Technologies Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: The Labor Market Remains Too Strong For Fed Easing
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this morning, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the employment data for the economy during the month of May. In May, the US economy created 139,000 jobs, which was slightly higher than expected. Over the last twelve months, the economy created 1.7 million jobs, which is consistent with the soft landing of the labor market. The unemployment rate remained at 4.2%. While the US labor market continues to remain quiet, there is no data on the employment side of the Federal Reserve’s dual mandate to suggest that monetary policy needs to move in any direction.

Leading Indicators Prior to Report

Like the market, I was surprised to see a stronger job creation number this morning as prior to this morning’s report, new data presented this week showed the labor market was still cool, and the picture was mixed. The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April Job Openings and Labor Survey (JOLTS) showing increased activity in the labor market. Hires and separations both rose in the month of April, but continue to remain below their pre-pandemic numbers, signaling that flows in the labor market (hiring, firing, quitting) are continuing at a slow pace.

Job openings rose in the month of April, bucking a mostly downward trend over the past couple of years. When compared to unemployment data, the economy continues to have more job openings than unemployed people, although the number is very close to parity, versus a shortage of over 5 million people in the labor force during the summer of 2022.

In terms of data during the month of May, weekly jobless claims pointed to a slowing picture in the labor market. First-time jobless claims rose above 245,000 last week, which is one of the highest levels in the past year. The four-week moving average for initial claims has steadily risen from under 225,000 to over 235,000 in less than two months.

On a continuing basis, ongoing jobless claims, which measure the number of workers still unemployed, rose to above 1.9 million during the month of May, which is at their highest level in at least a year. The four-week moving average has charged up by nearly 40,000 people over the last two months. While the jobless claims data doesn’t flash recession, it certainly hints at a growing base of unemployed people.

Several Indicators in the May Jobs Report Show Strength in the Labor Market

There are several items that investors should feel good about with respect to the strength of the labor market in the May employment report. First, the unemployment rate remains 0.2% below the Federal Reserve’s estimate for 2025. Additionally, the Fed’s long-term or projected level of unemployment at full employment is 4.2%, right where the current indicator is, suggesting the economy is at full employment.

Along with the regular unemployment measure, the U6 rate, which includes part-time workers seeking full-time work, remained at 7.8% for the month of May. While the U6 has slowly edged higher, it matches with the normalization of the labor market as the U6U3 spread (which measures the additional slack in the labor force) appears to be holding at late 2024 levels.

Trends in unemployment also saw improvement in the average duration of unemployment, which fell by nearly 1.5 weeks to 21.8 weeks in the month of May. Additionally, the number of people unemployed for more than 27 weeks dropped by more than 200,000 to 1.46 million. These declines are coming off concerning elevations, so I would classify the improvement as normalization versus strength.

What to Watch For – Changes in Labor Force Size

One of the reasons we’ve seen the unemployment rate stay below expectations has been the number of people leaving the labor force. In May alone, 800,000 adults left the labor force, as the total number of adults not in the labor force approached 103 million. In the last 12 months, nearly 2.5 million adults have left the labor force. When people leave the labor force (usually via retirement), it pushes the economy to hire replacements and shrinks the number of unemployed people. Should many of these individuals re-enter the labor force for any reason, the number of unemployed people will rise, and with it the unemployment rate.

Conclusion

The labor market continues to operate at a level consistent with full employment, with the unemployment rate remaining slightly lower than the Fed’s 2025 target. The Federal Reserve meets in less than two weeks to decide interest rates and issue new economic projections. I don’t believe the labor market can influence any change in monetary policy, and I believe the Federal Reserve’s economic projections regarding the labor market will remain the same.

data.title: May CPI Preview: Can You Even Trust The Data?
data.description:
The consensus

The U.S. Bureau of Labor is set to release the CPI inflation data for May on Wednesday, June 11th. The analyst consensus expectations are that:

  • The core CPI inflation will increase by 0.3% MoM, which is above the monthly inflation rate in April at 0.2%, and due to the base effect, the annual Core CPI is expected to increase from 2.8% to 3%.
  • The headline CPI inflation will increase also by 0.2% MoM, at the same rate as in April, which would keep the annual headline CPI inflation unchanged at 2.3%.

Thus, the expectations are that the core CPI inflation will remain well above the Fed's 2% target, while the headline inflation is expected to be near the 2% target. Note, that the Fed is more focused on the core CPI inflation, which means that inflation is still a problem.

In fact, due to the base effects, the core CPI inflation is expected to spike over the next 3 months to 3.3-3.5% if the monthly inflation readings remain at 0.3%.

Specifically, as the chart below shows, the inflation readings over the next three months will replace the very low monthly core inflation readings from the same period in 2024.

Inflation likely to surprise to the upside

However, it is very likely that the actual inflation readings over the next few months will be higher than expected, specifically due to the tariffs in place.

Specifically, these are the current tariffs in place, that are expected to boost inflation:

  • The universal 10% tariff on all imports - this should affect the prices of all imported goods
  • The 30% tariff on all imports from China - expected to affect the prices of all goods imported from China
  • The additional 25% tariff on autos, which is expected to affect the prices of imported cars, domestically produced cars with foreign parts, but also used cars
  • First 25%, and then a 50% tariff on steel and aluminum, which is expected to affect the prices of all goods using these inputs in production.

Thus, it is likely that inflation figures could be higher than expected. If the core CPI comes at 0.4%, the annual inflation is likely to spike above 4% by the end of 2025.

Note, that companies are already stating that there will be a 50% pass-through to consumers within three months. Unlike in 2017, this time companies are expected to absorb around 50% of tariffs and pass the other 50% to consumers via higher prices. Given that the labor market is still solid, it is likely that the consumer will allow for this increase without affecting the demand, which almost guarantees higher inflation.

Can you trust the data?

However, according to the Wall Street Journal, economists are increasingly concerned that the CPI data is not accurate.

Specifically, it seems like due to the cuts in spending, the government agency that reports the CPI inflation data BLS is short of staff needed to accurately collect and process the inflation data.

Thus, the quality of CPI inflation data is questionable. The CPI data collection is done by so-called "enumerators," who basically go to stores, offices, businesses, and others to collect the prices of goods and services—in different geographical regions. This data is then aggregated and reported as the CPI inflation, by category.

Now, due to the cuts, there are fewer enumerators, and as a result, some geographical areas are not covered, and some products are not directly measured. Thus, the new data aggregation uses increasingly a price guessing method called “different-cell imputation.”

This means that the reported CPI data will be more or less a guess of what the true CPI inflation is—and that's a major problem.

Now, economists don't think that this is an attempt to manipulate the data. However, there is no way of knowing whether the reported data is influenced, given that it is more or less a guess.

Thus, the reported actual data could actually be lower than expected if it's influenced. So, the problem is whether the CPI data be trusted for the purpose of investing and also policymaking.

Calm before storm

It's important to note that inflation is now largely at the Fed's target, based on the quarterly annualized inflation readings. The headline CPI is actually at 1.7%, while the core CPI is at 2.36%.

Thus, the Fed should be thinking about cutting interest rates at this point, and these should be "the goods cuts."

However, these inflation figures don't reflect the currently imposed tariffs in place, as previously discussed. Even more importantly, tariffs are expected to increase, as more sector-based tariffs will be likely imposed (pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, airline parts...), and also some reciprocal tariffs are likely to be imposed after the 90-day delay expires in July and August for China.

As a result, we are currently calm before the tariff-based induced inflationary storm. Inflation is likely to spike over the next 12 months.

Implications

Inflation is expected to spike over the next few months due to the tariffs in place, and over the next year if additional tariffs are imposed.

Thus, obviously, this is a negative for the Bond market, as long-term rates (TLT) are expected to increase, with the 10-Year Treasury yields (US10Y) rising above 5%—especially if Trump's tax cuts blow up the deficits.

In addition, the Fed will not be able to cut interest rates, even if the economy slows, which is also expected. Specifically, the net effect of the Trump policies (immigration, DOGE, and tariffs) is anti-growth. Thus, the economy is likely to slip into stagflation.

Stagflation is a worst-case scenario for the stock market—the economy is likely to slow, and possibly contract, while high inflation will prevent the Fed from cutting interest rates. Given that the S&P 500 (SP500) is still trading at bubble-like valuations, with the P/E ratio at 24, my outlook is highly negative—the bubble is facing stagflation.

However, the recent bounce in the stock market continues, and it's led again by the AI theme and the mega-cap tech stocks, like Nvidia (NVDA). The bubble could continue to inflate as long as the hard data does not confirm the stagflation.

So, now we are back at a discussion about the trust in the quality of the reported data. If the reported CPI cannot be trusted, and the reported data is lower than it should be, the stock market and the bond market could be distorted. If that's the case, and the Fed is pressured into cutting interest rates by the "low-quality data," these distortions could be magnified. We could be flying blind here.

data.title: Peloton Interactive: Sell Rated As The Business Is Likely To Stay Weak In The Near-Term
data.description:
Investment Overview

I give a sell rating for Peloton Interactive (NASDAQ:PTON) as I believe the business is going to continue seeing negative revenue growth. Hardware sales are collapsing, subscriber losses are accelerating, and the macro environment remains unsupportive. Execution weakness and unresolved operational inefficiencies are making things worse, which makes a meaningful recovery unlikely in the near term.

Business Description

PTON is branded as a connected fitness company, where they incorporate hardware, software, and content to create a premium at-home workout experience. Viewed simply, PTON's primary sales model is to sell the hardware (e.g., the Bike), followed by upselling users to subscribe to it. Through a monthly membership, users gain access to a library of live and on-demand classes across cycling, running, strength, yoga, and more. The hardware business is the lower-margin one, and the recurring subscriptions revenue stream is the high-margin profit driver.

Macroeconomic Pressures Demand Outlook

I believe the macro backdrop remains a major overhang for PTON. While fitness spending may be resilient in general, the appetite for such spending is not universal. PTON’s high-priced hardware still falls into the discretionary category, and it is not a category that does well in a weak macro environment. Management itself has flagged softness in sales in late 3Q25 and early April, which to me says that underlying demand is poor and has not seen any meaningful signs of recovery. PTON's operating metrics evidently show that it is hurt by this macro environment. Revenue continues to decline—down 2/9/13% in 1/2/3Q25. But the real red flag is that connected fitness product revenue fell 27% y/y in 3Q25, which is a steep and troubling drop. Subscriber losses are also piling up, with 3Q25 marking the fifth straight quarter of contraction and the sharpest decline yet at -6% y/y.

As I mentioned above, the hardware is the main point of “entry” for PTON to convert consumers into members, which brings in high-margin recurring revenue. When hardware sales collapse, that funnel dries up. Also, the fact that PTON needs to push for refurbished unit sales suggests demand for new products just isn’t there anymore. The weakness in selling hardware and converting buyers to subscribers also has long-term implications on growth, as it means PTON has a smaller base of customers to monetize.

For PTON to see a positive inflection here, the macro conditions have to turn for the better, and I don’t think that is going to materialize anytime soon.

App Strategy Is Not Working

On top of the above, it also does not help to know that PTON’s app strategy is not working as well as it should. For info, paid Connect Fitness subscribers are users who own a Peloton-connected fitness product. Paid app subscribers are those who do not own a connected fitness product but access it through the PTON app. The idea is this: it can significantly reduce the adoption hurdle for PTON content. It works well on paper, but there are signs that this strategy is simply not working.

Firstly, the downward revision to FY25 paid app subscription guidance (now reflecting a 30,000 drop at the midpoint) is the most obvious red flag. The reason provided was due to limiting media spend and investment in attracting new corporate wellness clients. But in my view, if subscriber strength is dependent on “media” spend”—which I take as marketing—then it speaks very poorly on the organic demand for this product. This has huge implications. If a low-friction, low-cost product like this struggles to attract and retain users without aggressive marketing, it just shows that the app is not appealing to users. Then it raises the question of how strong the entire PTON subscription ecosystem is.

This draws back to the sustainability of PTON’s entire growth engine. If the app is not appealing, then consumers are really just buying expensive hardware, which is just not an attractive business (there are a lot of competitors selling the same hardware product). The pullback in the Corporate Wellness channel (note this was previously positioned as a key growth lever) also suggests the initiative failed to gain traction.

Taken together, these moves paint a picture of a brand that is struggling to broaden its reach.

Operational Inefficiencies and Tech Debt Create a Drag on Profitability

PTON is not only facing external issues, but there are also a lot of issues internally. The biggest concern I have is that PTON appears to be operating in a very inefficient manner. For instance, PTON needed to shut down warehouses for three days each quarter just to conduct manual inventory counts. This simply doesn’t make any sense given that PTON is a business that is worth $2.8 billion. I would’ve expected PTON to be using a top-notch inventory tracking system to facilitate the business. The current way of doing things may have worked when PTON was small and scaling, but when it is this big ($2.5 billion in sales), it shows up in the P&L and is a headwind to margins. I see this as a “tech debt.”

Aside from these, other “tech debts” include the lack of proper diagnostic tools. Support agents often have to send technicians for multiple in-home visits, and this not only frustrates members, but also drives up the entire service cost as more labor hours are needed. These are not issues that management is not aware of. They know these are a source of churn, but nothing has really changed despite them knowing it.

Put together, these inefficiencies are major hurdles to execution, and any meaningful fix will likely require heavy investment—something that could weigh on margins.

Valuation

When I compare PTON to its peers in the broader fitness and equipment industry, I can see why this is a stock that will continue to trade at a discount. Here are some peers for comparison. Garmin has an expected revenue growth outlook of high single digits and trades at 18x forward EBITDA. Technogym Spa has a low-teens revenue growth CAGR outlook and trades at 11x forward EBITDA (a discount to GRMN because it has a much lower adj. EBITDA margin of ~19% vs. GRMN at 29%). Logically, a company with lower growth and margin than these numbers should trade at a discount, and indeed, PTON is a business I expect to see negative revenue growth for in the near term, with that view in mind, I also don’t think PTON will flip into generating meaningful profits anytime soon. Consensus numbers reflect this. They expect PTON to see negative revenue growth and continue to stay in negative EBITDA territory. Hence, I believe PTON, which trades at ~9.5x today, is at a justified discount to peers.

Risk

Internally, if management can make good progress in streamlining operations, it could be a real boost to margins. PTON is also considering raising subscription prices for the first time in over three years, and while I believe this will elevate churn, if it doesn’t, it could provide a meaningful uplift to margins. Also, while the connected fitness product segment is currently seeing demand headwinds, any signs of broader macro recovery could reignite demand.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for PTON. Not only is the macro situation bad for PTON, but also its brand appears to have fading consumer relevance, the business operates inefficiently, and I do not see a clear fix to all these. Both hardware and software demand are deteriorating, and internal “tech debt” will likely require material investment to resolve.

data.title: American Vanguard Corporation 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by American Vanguard Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: ASICS: Valuation Stays High Despite Moderate Guidance
data.description:
Introduction

ASICS Corporation (OTCPK:ASCCF) (OTCPK:ASCCY) is a leading Japanese sportswear company renowned for its performance running shoes and lifestyle brands. In 2024, the company reported both top-line and bottom-line expansion, coupled with new product launches, geographic diversification, and a rebound in some key regions.

With all of that said, the market seems to already price in this performance. The shares are trading at an elevated P/E multiple of 38.2x and an EV/EBITDA ratio of 19.6x. That puts ASICS at a premium to some of its more established international peers. So, while execution remains strong, the valuation does not provide a margin of safety. Therefore, I'm assigning a Hold rating.

Global Footprint and Brand Portfolio

ASICS is no longer regionally anchored as a running shoe business — it's now a globally diversified player with a balanced geography and product mix. The largest revenue contribution comes from Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) at 28% as of FY2024, which is followed by North America with 21%, Greater China with 16%, and Japan with 15%. The remainder comes from Southeast Asia, Oceania, and Latin America. No single geography dominates, which is in a way a natural hedge against a local slowdown. If North America slows down, either China or Southeast Asia can pick up the slack. It's unusual in this category to see this kind of equilibrium.

Business-wise, the performance-running segment dominates, generating almost half of ASICS’s business and remaining the firm’s strongest category — based on competitive technology and years of R&D. What’s new in more recent times, though, is how other segments balance around the main segment. SportStyle and Onitsuka Tiger now account for almost 30% of business and are growing faster than the legacy businesses. These product lines are targeted at fashion-centric, youth-oriented shoppers — and the data suggests they’re picking up momentum. This is an important pivot because it's no longer just a niche brand for runners.

Another important development is the direct-to-consumer channel (DTC) distribution channel. ASICS continues to pick up additional volume in its retail and e-commerce stores. It’s important for two key reasons: margins and brand image. Being closer to the end consumer gives ASICS better data, better management of inventory, and better control over the customer experience. Add all these things up, and you’ve got a performance-running business with more legs to stand on.

FY2024 and Q1 2025 Results

ASICS reported a strong FY2024. Revenue rose 18.9% year over year to ¥678.5 billion, with sound contribution across all core markets. Operating income rose 84.7% to ¥100.1 billion, and net income rose 80.9% to ¥63.8 billion. Those are not just marginal gains — they signal highly effective operating discipline and cost-control measures. The gross margin rose from 49.5% to 52.3%, and the operating margin rose more than 500 basis points to 14.8%. For this category brand, this kind of gross-margin expansion is rare — and shows the product-mix transition (focusing on Tiger and DTC) is paying off.

Metric FY2024 FY2023 YoY Change
Revenue ¥678.5 billion ¥570.4 billion 18.9%
Performance Running ¥326.9 billion ¥285.9 billion 14.3%
Core Performance Sports ¥78.6 billion ¥72.1 billion 9%
Apparel and Equipment ¥38.0 billion ¥36.1 billion 5.2%
SportStyle ¥98.4 billion ¥59.2 billion 66.1%
Onitsuka Tiger ¥95.4 billion ¥60.3 billion 58.3%
Gross Profit ¥378.8 billion ¥296.8 billion 18.9%
Operating Income ¥100.1 billion ¥54.2 billion 84.7%
Net income ¥63.8 billion ¥35.2 billion 80.9%

Geographically, the growth was strong in all key regions. Greater China led the pack with 29.6% expansion, with Asia-Pacific ("Others") following closely at 21.7%. Japan, Europe, and North America each expanded in the mid-teens; the portfolio had no weak points. By category, performance running business was as strong as ever — but it was Onitsuka Tiger and lifestyle brands that stood out. Tiger alone expanded by 58% year-over-year. Not only is this good for the top line — it's also margin-accretive as well.

The balance sheet also strengthened this year. ASICS closed Q1 2025 with ¥113.5 billion in cash and cash equivalents, while the inventories stayed in check. Capex was also modest, and the group raised its year-end dividend by 20% to ¥20 per share. The management did not overreach, but it is certainly bullish in terms of the sustainability of earnings.

Looking at the Q1 2025, the momentum seems to be aligned in the same direction. The full-year consolidated figures are yet to be announced, but the region-by-region figures indicate tailwinds. Greater China and Southeast Asia regions continue showing strength, and new products like the Gel-Nimbus 26 are selling well.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Revenue ¥208.3 billion ¥174.1 billion 19.7%
Performance Running ¥98.0 billion ¥87.8 billion 11.5%
Core Performance Sports ¥25.4 billion ¥24.8 billion 2.5%
Apparel and Equipment ¥10.3 billion ¥9.3 billion 10.9%
SportStyle ¥35.1 billion ¥23.4 billion 49.6%
Onitsuka Tiger ¥28.3 billion ¥18.0 billion 57.2%
Gross Profit ¥116.3 billion ¥94.8 billion 22.6%
Operating Income ¥44.5 billion ¥33.8 billion 31.6%
Net Income ¥31.6 billion ¥26.7 billion 18.4%

ASICS also reaffirmed its guidance for FY2025. This year, they expect revenues of ¥780 billion (+15%) and net income of around ¥78 billion (+22.2%). To me, this signals that management is being conservative, and they don't expect any major catalysts anytime soon. Given that the company is currently trading at elevated multiples, this doesn't scream "bullish".

So in short, the business is doing well. The expansion is steady, margins are heading in the right direction, and the company appears to be well-balanced. Not much to complain about — maybe just the price.

Valuation: Elevated Multiples With a Moderate Guidance

ASICS trades at a 3.68x sales, 38.2x trailing P/E, and 19.61x EV/EBITDA — far ahead of its comparables and sector median (18.71x P/E). In FY2024, the company reported 18.9% revenue and 80.9% net income growth, reaffirming its FY2025 guidance. If we assume that they will perform just in line with guidance, that puts the forward P/E at roughly 32.8x. These multiples indicate that the market is expecting flawless execution with no leeway for mistakes.

Company P/S P/E (TTM) EV/EBITDA Revenue Growth (YoY) Net Income Growth (YoY)
ASICS 3.68 38.20 19.61 18.9% 80.9%
NIKE, Inc. (NKE) 1.95 20.69 16.18 0.3% 12.4%
On Holding AG (ONON) 6.62 82.25 55.14 29.4% 204.4%
adidas AG (OTCQX:ADDYY) 1.66 39.76 21.23 10.5% 1118.7%
PUMA SE (OTCPK:PMMAF) 0.40 18.12 8.86 2.5% -7.6%
Skechers U.S.A., Inc. (SKX) 1.0 14.89 9.99 12.1% 17.2%
Birkenstock Holding plc (BIRK) 4.90 37.98 18.28 21.0% 155.4%
Crocs, Inc. (CROX) 1.45 6.28 6.80 3.5% 19.9%
Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) 3.23 16.72 11.34 16.3% 27.2%
Steven Madden, Ltd. (SHOO) 0.77 10.69 6.64 15.2% -1.3%

On a price-to-sales multiple, ASICS is ahead of Skechers (1.0x), Nike (1.95x), and Adidas (1.66x) but in line with higher-growth comps like Deckers (3.23x). Its top-line progress, however, is considerably healthier than that of the sector average (2.31%) and in closer alignment with lifestyle peers like Birkenstock (21.0%) and On Holding (29.4%).

In terms of EV/EBITDA, the situation is not that different. ASICS's 19.61x EV/EBITDA puts it closer to the likes of Adidas (21.23x) and Birkenstock (18.28x), but further away from the slower-growth peers like Skechers (9.99x), Puma (8.86x), and Crocs (6.80x). Deckers, one of the industry leaders, is trading at only 11.34x EV/EBITDA despite having comparable top-line growth and similar gross margins.

The P/E ratio reflects a similar image. On a trailing P/E of 38.2x and a forward P/E of 32.8x, ASICS is priced ahead of lower-growth, more established players like Nike (20.69x) and Skechers (14.89x) and on par with fashion and hype-driven players like Birkenstock (37.98x) and On Holding (82.25x). It's also close to Adidas (39.76x), which is recovering nicely from a ghastly FY2023.

Overall, ASICS is being priced as a soundly managed, margin-expanding international brand. However, with forward expectations already priced in the multiple, the upside from here can only come from guidance upgrades or significant outperformance.

Catalysts: Growth Drivers Still in Play

ASICS has a number of tailwinds heading into 2025. One of those is the popularity of its Onitsuka Tiger brand, which grew by 58% in FY2024. It is really popular in China and Southeast Asia, and if this trend continues, it can impact top-line and bottom-line growth.

The company is also showing an improvement through direct-to-consumer (DTC) website and store sales. These are way more profitable since ASICS is selling goods straight to consumers instead of third-party retailers. As this becomes a bigger part of the company, it should continue to drive higher margins.

International expansion is another area of growth. ASICS is experiencing heightened demand in emerging regions like China and Southeast Asia and continues to expand in these regions. Since there is plenty of room left in emerging markets, it is likely to be a contributing factor to the business.

There is also a short-term boost from the global events. ASICS sponsors national teams and athletes, and with the run-up to the 2026 World Cup, it should add some extra exposure. These types of events often lead to a pickup in sales, especially for new or limited-edition products.

Lastly, dividends and cash are helping. ASICS increased its dividend by 20% and holds a cash pile of over ¥113 billion. This ensures that the company can return money to shareholders in the form of dividends or share buybacks — keeping the current share price in check.

Risks: Expectations leave no room for failure

ASICS is priced on strong execution — and that is a risk by itself. Based on the current multiples of 38x trailing and 32.8x forward P/E, the stock is pricing in continuous top-line and bottom-line expansion, higher margins, and good execution. If FY2025 results come in weaker than expected or guidance is not met, the valuation multiples could compress quickly.

Additionally, there is a risk related to fashion trends. The Onitsuka Tiger brand is a significant driver of growth, but trends are transitory — especially in the youth-related segments. If the brand starts losing momentum or the market becomes saturated, it could put pressure on both sales and margins.

Competition is another major risk in the shoe business. Nike, Adidas, On Holding, and other popular brands are all expanding and chasing the trends. Even if ASICS has a specialty in the performance running segment, it does not have the same level of global market presence as some other brands.

Currency risk is also an important risk to consider. Having over 85% of sales in regions outside of Japan, an appreciating yen would reduce reported sales and profit. Although partially hedged, ASICS remains exposed to FX volatility — most notably with raw material costs and global operations.

Additionally, supply chain-related disruptions are also present. The company is heavily reliant on Asian manufacturing, so any political tensions, supply chain hiccups, or tariffs can significantly impact the numbers.

Lastly, consumer spending is a serious macro-related risk. ASICS is in the business of selling discretionary goods, so if consumer demand weakens in major markets, like the U.S. or Europe, this could lead to higher discounts and lower margins — exactly when the market expects flawless execution.

Conclusion

In recent years, ASICS has done exceptionally well. The company diversified its product mix, ventured into new geographies, and kept the operational execution at the highest level. If we look at the financials, margins are widening, the Onitsuka Tiger brand is expanding, and the organization seems better-rounded than ever before.

However, the main issue is valuation. When the stock is trading at 38.2x trailing and 32.8x forward earnings, a lot of optimism is priced in. Unless the company dramatically beats analyst estimates or significantly increases its guidance, there is no room for multiple expansions. Therefore, I'm rating ASICS stock a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: A Tale Of Two Teslas
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Musk vs. Trump and how it's affecting Tesla (0:25). Broadcom, CoreWeave and AI stocks (8:05). ON Semiconductor movements (12:15). Dollar General earnings and Zscaler's follow through (13:45). Tariff updates, CPI data, and Powell pressure (18:00).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, Seeking Alpha's Director of news. Welcome back to the podcast. Always great to talk to you on these Wall Street Roundups.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here. Looking forward to it, thanks.

RS: We were discussing what might be the top story of the week, a lot of stories in contention. But perhaps Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), everybody's favorite underfollowed stock and favorite underfollowed CEO and favorite underfollowed topic.

What did Tesla show us this week? A lot of discussion about Musk and DOGE and Tesla and whether it it can recover. What did we see out of Tesla this week?

BS: Quiet reserves, unassuming Elon Musk somehow found himself in the news this week. I mean, if you're looking at it from just sort of a drama perspective, obviously, can't help but talk about the turn that's happened.

It's Musk versus Trump now. Elon Musk has come out against the the bill, the budget bill that's currently passed the House. It's now working its way through the senate.

Musk's main con point of contention seems to be around the, electric vehicle provisions in the bill. Specifically, there was incentives to purchase tax incentives to purchase electric vehicles in the old law and this gets rid of that.

Musk is taking a a broader view in his public commentary, talking about the additional the debt impact from the spending bill, which obviously is opposed to the DOGE goals that he was chasing not too long ago.

So I think for Tesla as a stock, it's an interesting turning point, interesting next step in this journey. Tesla obviously has had wide swings so far this year, largely based on kind of the political fate of its CEO.

There was the narrative being formed was that Musk's return to Tesla after his DOGE adventure was good news for the stock and the stock was now going to be able to get back to normal.

And then that CEO instantly picks a fight with the President that he had supported just a few weeks ago. So it's difficult to to to see where the branding situation goes from here.

There had been talk that Tesla, even though it faced a lot of backlash from Musk's political activities recently, that it was building in a Trump premium that fans of Donald Trump would rally to Tesla as a symbol of that side of the political spectrum.

If Musk's going to take on Trump then that premium might disappear, which I mean, maybe that just leaves Tesla as a normal company again, kind of xing out the situation that happened earlier this year, or maybe it leaves Tesla with fewer fans generally. It remains to be seen.

RS: Well, everybody knows the whole world's a stage, but did you anticipate - last week, I asked you if you anticipated some of the tariff conversation movements out of the courts, out of Trump.

Did anybody expect or was this expected, this jawboning between Musk and Trump? Is this part of the drama? Is this part of the play? Any sense, is there a clear cut answer here or is this part of the play that we find ourselves in as the audience?

BS: Just tracking the personalities, I think this is pretty predictable. You've got two people who think pretty highly of their opinion, two people who have their own base of power.

They don't rely on each other in any way. Musk being the richest man in the world, Trump being President of the United States.

They don't really need to be allies in any sense. They're also both both people who don't mind a public spat, who aren't known for their consistency in terms of their rhetoric and their goals from moment to moment.

So the idea that they would be aligned for a period and then be in opposition for a period, I don't think it's strange at all. I also wouldn't think it was strange if they made amends and were aligned again at some point in the future. So I don't see this necessarily as a a permanent rift. I don't even know if this will have a lasting impact on Tesla.

What might have a lasting impact, though, is the actual provisions of the spending bill as it works its way through Congress where that lands might might affect Tesla's business.

I think from an investor's point of view, you have to think about it as there being two Teslas. And one Tesla is the car company, and that business is certainly in flux.

There's a lot of uncertainty surrounding that. Obviously, there was some brand damage done. I'm especially thinking about in Europe where sales were down pretty sharply while Musk was doing DOGE.

Meanwhile, if you're losing the tax support you were getting for selling electric vehicles, that will impact the possibilities for sales moving forward. Tesla was already facing some market share pressure from just the proliferation of EVs and EV makers.

The other Tesla though is the the broader tech company. So autonomous vehicles, robotics, the other projects that Tesla likes to announce and then work towards over time.

If you're still a big believer in that promise and those further horizons that really have nothing to do with the car company except the car company is the the launching pad for those future technologies, then it doesn't really matter what the the spending bill has to say about EV support or what Musk's current association is with the President of the United States.

You're betting for five, ten, fifteen years in the future, and you're betting on trillion dollar markets. So I think it really comes down to who you are as an investor.

Are you are you a believer in that long term vision? Do you think that that's gonna happen? Or are you buying into a car company, in which case, I think the current situation becomes much more relevant.

RS: Do you have the sense that there is more strategy behind this timing and this jawboning and this public disagreement?

Do you think from both sides, from both Trump and Musk, do you think that there's more strategy involved in - you talk about a tale of two Teslas.

Are there a tale of two Musks? Are there a tale of two Trumps in terms of what they're strategizing for behind closed doors and how they're making that known in front of people?

BS: There could be. Certainly, on Musk's part, if he had come to the conclusion or had been convinced by leadership at Tesla that the association with the administration was ultimately near term losing or they'd already kind of had the benefit they were gonna get from that or it was just becoming a pure distraction.

There could be an upside to making a very obvious rake, not just, hey, I'm gonna set this aside and go back to my day job, but literally saying I am no longer part of this administration. In fact, now we have beef.

I could see a strategic component to it. It's hard to say because every action Musk does, you can interpret strategically, you can interpret as aggressive behavior from a person who has the resources to do whatever he wants.

So I don't know. I think we're obviously separated from his thinking, so it's hard to make a definitive judgment. And I think in terms of, if you're trying to guess from the outside looking in, it's kind of in the eye of the beholder.

RS: Something that has been a thread through these conversations and also through many conversations on our Investing Experts podcast has been this notion about AI and who the top players may be at the end of one year, two year, five years, and and how the, evolution of the AI conversation will advance.

Some of those prominent names that we've seen in the past couple of weeks make a lot of market movements, be up, be down for consecutive days, and that we've heard a lot of bullish sentiment and some bearish sentiment on our Investing Experts podcast.

Two names has have been CoreWeave (CRWV), which IPO'd a few months ago, and Broadcom (AVGO).

I think that those might be two specific stocks to highlight from the AI conversation. Broadcom is releasing earnings after we speak today, so it's a bit preemptive, but I think still some insight to be had.

And, also, CoreWeave, they've been down today but had repeated consecutive sessions of gains. How would you contextualize the moves in those two stocks? Do they deserve to be part of the same conversation? What are your thoughts there?

BS: So Broadcom, as you point out, is reporting after the close today. We're recording this on Thursday. So the listeners are gonna know more about the specific earnings than we are talking about it now.

However, I will note that the stock has been up seven days in a row going into today, Thursday. It was up fractionally when I checked a little earlier. So if it finishes higher, that's eight days in a row. So that's a lot of enthusiasm, a lot of optimism going into the earnings report.

So I think, speaking to the people of the future, it'll be interesting to see to what extent Broadcom was able to fulfill that promise, going into it.

And I think in terms of beyond the headline numbers for Broadcom, I think look to the commentary from executives about the buying behavior of the big players that Broadcom is associated with.

So Apple (AAPL), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), those are the kind of clients that Broadcom has. And so if they have any sort of information on where that is headed in the near term, I think that'll be interesting for the sector as a whole.

And CoreWeave, I think, is interesting to keep track of just because it's the most prominent AI driven IPO that's come out recently.

So I think, probably the early stages of a wave of companies that are gonna come to market specifically, meant to or situated to appeal to AI investors.

So this is next generation, stalwarts like Nvidia (NVDA) who took advantage of the early wave of AI interest.

And so CoreWeave at last check was down about 16% as we're talking on Thursday, but that's coming off of four days of gains, including a 25%, rise on Tuesday. This is all pretty much momentum play. The stock continues to build new post IPO gains. It's up nearly 250% since IPO.

It's sitting at about just below 140. It IPO'd at 40 back in March. Another interesting aspect of CoreWeave that we've mentioned when we talked about it before is it didn't have a lot of enthusiasm in that initial run.

Like, you'll see IPOs that pop significantly on the first day and then kinda drift lower or or kind of move sideways. CoreWeave was a much more delayed response from the market. It came public in kind of a whimper and then built momentum.

I think that is an interesting model to look at when you're looking at the way in which AI investors and tech investors generally are looking for new ideas.

RS: Speaking of tech investors, one another, stock that saw some movements that's peripherally related to this conversation is ON Semiconductor (ON). What would you say about that stock and its place in this conversation around tech stocks?

BS: ON Semiconductor rallied earlier this week. It's had some executives speaking at an investor conference. They were very upbeat. They said a recovery is beginning and they think Q2 will be the bottom. I mean, the key to that obviously is to be a bottom, you've got to have trouble. That's happened in the past. ON Semiconductor has been facing an inventory glut, underutilized capacity. It wasn't well positioned for the the coming tech market, the market that emerged.

So executives are starting to get to the point where they think that turnaround is gonna happen. I think it's noteworthy that their main markets are the industrial and automotive markets. So these are old economy customers.

So it underlines the idea that even within the tech space, you have to look at these companies as individuals and see what their markets are, who their customers are, what they're targeting, especially as you are looking to determine if there are other players who haven't received their AI premium yet.

That ticket hasn't been stamped yet. So as you're looking around to the next Nvidia, it's important to get under the hood of these companies and see where they are situated in the market.

RS: Along with the tech sector, another thread that has been pretty consistent has been the retail space, and we've been talking about it for the past couple weeks more prominently. But a lot to be uncovered around the tariff conversation.

And a stock that reported this week that saw a nice rise post earnings was Dollar General (DG). What would you say about what they have going on, what their earning showed?

We had some recent news with China and the US today. What would you say about the tariff conversations, the retail picture, and what Dollar General may have showed investors this past week?

BS: So Dollar General jumped 16% on Tuesday on earnings news.

It's interesting just because retailers don't tend to to move like that, especially an old school one like Dollar General. So it's kind of a tech move for a non tech company.

The company beat expectations, raised its forecast. It would talk specifically about the tariffs as you had pointed out, noting that it's still an uncertain situation, but it's taking steps to lower its reliance on China.

The stock had dropped last August and in the wake of a disastrous earnings report, it had lowered guidance at that point and fell precipitously.

It's still below where it was before that earnings report came out, but the gains recently plus the improvement that it's been seeing in the past few months has put Dollar General stock back to where it was for the first time since that August decline.

I think if we're looking more broadly, a takeaway from it, whatever dislocation has come from the the tariff discussion and wherever we land on the final tariffs, it might be healthy for these companies to diversify their supply chains to some extent.

So a company like Dollar General going forward might keep in mind that it faces these international threats, whether it be from trade wars or, something like a COVID that would break down supply chains.

And COVID's a bad example because it broke down all supply chains, but imagine if there was an outbreak of a COVID like virus in a particular country that might affect some companies more than others.

And so I think having a little bit more diversity might be helpful. So it might be a sign that retailers are adjusting to the new environment and adjusting to the new environment in such a way that the uncertainty becomes baked in.

So they are creating supply chains that are more flexible, supply chains that aren't structured around any one particular country or any one particular region.

So you might end up with even among these seemingly, boring, for lack of a better word, stocks like Dollar General, you're getting to the point where they're becoming more nimble in response to these headline risks.

RS: And any other stocks you would add to the conversation around market movers this week and what it may portend for investors?

BS: This one's a little old at this point, but I just thought it was interesting to point out Zscaler (ZS). It was up about 10% last Friday on earnings.

It's also continued to rise since that rally. It's up again today as we're talking, and that would be five days in a row of gain. So it's seen a lot of follow through after that post earnings pop.

The upside's also helped it reach new highs. It's up about 68% year to date and 84% since it hit its April lows. It's a cloud security company, and it's been boosted by the rollout of its of new AI security offering.

It's just been a theme of the conversations we've been having the last few weeks of these next stages in the AI trade and the way that specific companies are applying AI to their products and the way they're interacting with customers.

And so it's just another example of a company that was able to leverage that innovation and see some benefit from it from the stock price.

RS: And macro wise, what are you most focused on or what have you been most focused on this past week, and what do we have to look forward to?

Obviously, as I mentioned, we had some news out of China today. I don't know how much that is figuring into things, or it's still a wait and see part of things. But where where are you at macro wise?

BS: I think the tariff conversations and the international situation are obviously going to be huge deciders in the near term day to day trading.

Like I said before, the Dollar General case, there are signs that companies are becoming more well tuned to the uncertainty. So not just not being as reliant on these these headlines to move around.

So you might see the market start to ignore some of these headlines or have more muted responses to them just with the understanding that they might not be indicative of of major macro changes and just become kind of a bit of the background noise.

Looking further ahead, next week we have CPI data coming out. This will be the latest in inflation. We talked about Trump versus Musk earlier, so we also have Trump versus Powell. Trump's been putting a lot of pressure on Fed Chairman Jerome Powell to lower interest rates.

Powell has obviously been quiet about it. He's not gonna get into a public spat with the President. But there were rumors earlier this week that the pressure might get so much that Powell will be forced to resign.

That hasn't happened yet. As I've said before, Powell is pretty good at ignoring the outside pressure from the to do things. Whatever criticisms people might have about Powell, they're not gonna say that he's overly responsive to either the market or public pressure.

So if anyone is well situated to ignore pleas from the President of the United States, I think Powell is that person.

Just checking the Fed generally, the meeting later this month, so in a couple weeks from now, there's a 97% chance of no cut based on market trading at this point.

Looking further along, there's a July meeting and there's a 69% chance that that rates are gonna stay the same through that meeting. So there's a consensus building now that September is the the most likely time for a cut though there's a reasonable chance that there might be one in July.

That keeps getting pushed back, like earlier this year, I think the expectation was we'd be significantly lower in rates by now than we are.

So there's still the expectation that rate cuts are coming, but over the course of, 2025 so far, that prospect is getting further pushed back based on the idea that that inflation might be stickier than we thought.

And so that makes the CPI report pretty important for sealing in that timing and that expectation just because any sign that inflation has finally tamed to the degree that the Fed doesn't have to think that hard about it, would open the door for those rate cuts to come sooner.

RS: There's been some rumors about Powell resigning before his time is up. Do you have any sense or anything worthy to note around when Powell may or may not be out of his role?

BS: I think that in in a lot of ways, that's a personal decision on his part. The Fed chairmanship isn't a political position the way a senator is or a governor, and I don't just mean that they're not directly elected.

Typically, after becoming Fed Chairman, former Fed Chairs just go to academia or write books or open up consulting businesses. Janet Yellen is an exception to that. She became treasury secretary, but by and large, Powell's not putting political capital in his back pocket for some later use, most likely.

So I don't know how much political pressure a President could put on him unless Powell himself chooses to receive that pressure. But that said, I could see how it would weigh on somebody to constantly be in the spotlight and constantly have that.

The burden of responsibility, again, Powell was Fed Chair during COVID, and I can't imagine more pressure and more responsibility than that. So my general feeling and again, this is completely outsider, I've no information whatsoever about his personal feelings on this, is just this does not seem to be the peak of pressure that Powell has felt in his career so far.

I just think this is most likely just roll off his back. That said, he's been chair for a while. It's a tough job. He might be getting to the point of his career where he'd rather, like I said, sit in a nice, cushy endowed chair at a high prestige college and books from there. So it really is gonna come down to where he stands and and what his personal goals are.

RS: Maybe he starts leading meditation courses in Bali. Who knows?

BS: Sure. That actually sounds nice. I think we pitch that to him. That might make his decision.

RS: Honestly, look at what he's survived and somewhat thrived through. It might be nice for him to give lessons on how to maintain your sanity through some insane situations, I would say.

BS: Yeah. I mean, Fed chairmanship is a tough gig. I mean, I'm thinking about just going through the names of Greenspan or Bernanke to Yellen, Powell.

There's never not a crisis. There's not a boring time to be Fed Chair, and you're the public face of this institution that I think most people don't even understand.

The rate cut debate is a good one. Whatever you think he should be doing, it's pretty easy for politicians to always want lower rates. I mean, that's what everyone - it's like giving out candy to kids. No kid's gonna say no. Please, let's have some carrots first.

And so, you're kind of the the bad parent in that way. You're more likely in the situation to say no, we have to have dinner first, and then we can have some dessert. So I don't know. It feels like it's a powerful job. In that respect, I can see why people are drawn to it, but it also feels thankless and just difficult.

Just like having to pick your words in public the way that a Fed Chair does and a way the Fed Chair no other position has to to do it. I mean, you can move markets just by leaving out a word that you had included in a similar phrase six months ago.

And there's just a lot of even moment by moment, like if he's in public and he's speaking, he has to literally think about every word that comes out of his mouth. He can't, just, in a public sparring match with Donald Trump, it's a difficult position to be in because Trump can say whatever he wants. Meanwhile, Powell has to be extremely precise about what he's saying.

And again, not that any Fed Chair would ever publicly speak out against the the sitting President. I can't remember something like that ever happening. If I was sitting down for a beer with Jerome Powell, I might tell him, yeah, maybe go do some meditation in Bali. That might be nice.

RS: Anything to highlight about what's coming up next week in terms of earnings or some stock specific news?

BS: So a couple big names coming out next week. We have Oracle (ORCL). It's up about 44% since its April low. However, it's still below its fifty two week high. It would need about another 15% rally from here to reach its previous 52 week high.

It's a company that's gotten a lot of attention for the extent to which it's rolling out AI powered products and using AI in its business. So, more commentary from that will be interesting.

This is kind of I think in the Zscaler camp and that camp of next wave AI winners. I mean, Oracle's already on that list, so that's already been talked about. But it'll be interesting to see where they stand.

On the same theme, Adobe (ADBE) is reporting next week as well. It's up, not as much as as Oracle, but up 26% from its April lows. There has been concerns about the monetization of its AI products.

So look for commentary about that, the extent to which the the AI innovations that it's putting into place, how directly and when are those gonna directly impact the bottom line?

Online retailers and the online commerce space. So just on that front, Chewy (CHWY) is reporting next week and so is Stitch Fix (SFIX). So if you've got an eye on on that sector, I think those are gonna be interesting stocks to talk about.

data.title: The Interpublic Group: Positioned For Structural Gains With $39 Price Target
data.description:
Restructuring Pains, Structural Gains: Strong Buy on IPG

We initiate The Interpublic Group of Companies, Inc. (NYSE:IPG) at Strong Buy, $39 PT. The Interpublic Group is a global advertising and marketing services provider via Integrated Agency Networks and IPG DXTRA, with a wide spectrum of capabilities across digital marketing, PR, and data-driven communications. Our conviction is based on three key tenets:

1) IPG's earnings profile is at/near a cyclical trough with our 2025 EPS of $2.17 (19% below consensus) based on continued organic headwinds (down 3.6% in 1Q), $203mn of restructuring, and management's conservative guide on margin recovery;

2) consensus includes aggressive 2025 cost-out benefits, while our timeline for meaningful margin expansion remains in 2026/beyond, supported by historical restructuring track record and management disclosure;

3) importantly, even on our deliberately conservative estimates, applying a 15X forward P/E (on 2026E vs. 17.3X today, a stretched multiple well above peers) provides a path to $39/share. Risks from protracted client account losses and slower organic growth remain, but this should be adequately reflected in valuation and our numbers.

With sentiment too focused on near-term pain, IPG's earnings power and cost discipline story offers compelling R/R, warranting clear action at current levels.

Liquidity Is King: IPG’s Balance Sheet Firepower Drives Shareholder Value Amid the Noise

IPG's balance sheet resilience represents a well of latent shareholder value to be unlocked, particularly as the company weathers cyclicality, restructuring charges, and a transformative merger with Omnicom. Ending Q1'25 with $1.87bn in cash & equivalents, total debt at $2.96bn, and no material leverage increase despite a net loss reported, the company's liquidity position is a bulwark. But it also is a lever: enabling IPG to continue capital returns even in a choppy environment, as evidenced by i) repurchasing 3.4mn shares for $90mn, and ii) paying $125.3mn in dividends; both in Q1. That the latter was maintained at $0.33/share despite near-term margin pressure, and the former continued despite a net loss quarter, speaks to the underlying financial discipline.

That discipline is about more than optics: restructuring charges, totaling $203.3mn, clearly impacted P&L, but management's willingness to continue capital returns signals confidence in FCF's sufficiency to fund both operations and ST commitments. Importantly, with a laddered debt maturity profile and leverage ratios well below covenant thresholds, IPG's balance sheet is poised for action: whether to accelerate integration investments as the Omnicom merger finalizes or to ramp up share repurchases should the stock stay priced for a different reality.

AI Platforms as a Strategic Engine: IPG data-led differentiation creates durable flywheel

IPG's AI-driven platform leadership is quickly changing the marketing services competitive landscape, setting the stage for transformational value creation in the decade ahead. The crown jewel is "Interact," IPG’s unified marketing operating system built on proprietary Acxiom data & AI, now fully rolled out to the agency portfolio to orchestrate all moments of the campaign lifecycle—from audience, to insights, to creative ideation, to real-time omnichannel activation, to commerce and CRM. This is not iterative innovation: Interact has consolidated data, engineering, martech, and adtech into one integrated infrastructure, breaking down legacy silos and unlocking “mass personalization” at scale, previously a more aspirational concept than operational reality.

The platform also infuses cutting-edge generative AI (Adobe GenStudio, Firefly, and other models) into the marketing cycle, directly accelerating velocity and relevance, enabling IPG to activate campaigns across all digital touchpoints with consumer preferences in mind, at a scale and speed that was unimaginable in legacy workflows. With a reported $100mn investment in the AI transformation, and strategic partnerships with Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Blackbird AI, IPG is not just responding to industry disruption; it’s setting the gold standard for how data, identity resolution, and AI can drive "mass personalization" and real-time optimization, translating directly to client outcomes and operating leverage.

IPG: Lower EPS Trajectory Reflects Fundamentally Conservative Outlook on Restructuring Realization and Persistent Topline Headwinds

We forecast Interpublic Group’s FY25E EPS at $2.17 (the street $2.64) assuming a conservative outlook on the timing of restructuring benefits and topline pressures. While The street models capitalize the full run-rate of ~$300-350mn targeted cost take-out in FY25, we assume $0.40–0.45/sh of restructuring/deal charges hit GAAP in the next four quarters, which are not fully embedded in Bloomberg estimates.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $8.71B 4 $8.94B 6
EBITDA $1.38B 4 $1.42B 6
Net Income $996.35M 4 $1.04B 6
EPS $2.64 6 $2.82 7
SG&A Expenses $78.02M 4 $80.08M 6

Further, IPG’s negative organic trajectory (Q1 org -3.6%; guided -1% to -2% FY25) and client losses (4.5-5% drag) make an immediate margin recovery difficult, and management guidance confirms a conservative outlook. We see authentic margin recovery only in late FY26E, ~1 year behind market expectations, consistent with prior restructuring cycles. In our view, the consensus is underappreciating the timing disconnect and magnitude of transitory GAAP headwinds, resulting in our FY25E/FY26E EPS estimates -19%/-8% below the street, respectively. We acknowledge the risk of reacceleration (faster cost take-out or org rebound), but our conviction is based on charge timing, which is non-embedded in The street models and supported by management disclosure and historical precedent.

Valuation

Our $39 price target for IPG is based on a forward P/E of 15.0x, applied to our FY26E $2.60 diluted EPS forecast, reflecting a deliberate downside buffer and upside leverage. Our target multiple, while below the current 17.3x and the 83rd percentile of IPG's historical range, still represents a significant premium to IPG's long-term median (14.1x) and to global peers (9.7x), which, we believe, is justified by IPG's ability to out-execute in a disrupted industry, the visibility into margin recovery following restructuring, and 19.8% FY26E EPS growth on conservative assumptions.

Our analysis — which incorporates management disclosures and the recently published 10-K — acknowledges the near-term headwinds (Q1:25 organic revenue down 3.6%, $203mn of restructuring charges) and recognizes that cost discipline and mix changes will ultimately drive the company to re-capture the premium earnings power at the median of its history over the medium term. While consensus models appear to price in margin recovery and cost savings in 2025, our view pushes real inflection into FY26E, and our target multiple therefore includes a prudent temporal risk premium that has seemed to be overlooked by peers and the street.

Critically, we see the mispricing not in near-term earnings, but rather in the market's persistent under-appreciation for IPG's repeatable restructuring discipline, which history suggests will ultimately unlock shareholder value at the sentiment trough. In our view, the valuation skepticism today offers a compelling entry point, as IPG's premium is supported by resilient fundamentals and proven management execution, rather than a reflection of optimism.

Risks to our Strong Buy thesis

Our Strong Buy thesis on IPG could be challenged if near-term restructuring doesn’t yield savings as expected or revenue declines are deeper and stickier than even management anticipates. While we believe the market has priced in pretty significant restructuring pain and the timing lag of cost take-outs, a protracted stretch of client attrition/inability to replace lost accounts (in healthcare & media which drove the 3.6% organic decline) could necessitate further downward revisions to earnings and impair the multiple re-rating we forecast.

Integration risk related to the Omnicom merger is also meaningful: if cost synergies take longer to materialize or if operational disruption results in talent/client losses, the margin recovery supporting our $39 target could be delayed or diluted, a la prior large-agency combinations. Tracking the quarterly pace of restructuring charges, the organic revenue trajectory across key agency segments & early signs of Omnicom integration progress (regulatory/cost-out run-rate) remains critical to assessing whether IPG can indeed execute the transition from trough earnings to structural gains. If cost discipline slips or merger execution stumbles, our call for medium-term multiple expansion on normalized EPS could prove overly bullish despite the depressed starting valuation.

Conclusion

We maintain our Strong Buy rating on IPG based on our belief that street pessimism on near-term restructuring and organic headwinds mispriced a durable earnings power and structural cost discipline. While the street may be overzealous in modeling margin recovery, we see best-in-class value creation potential as IPG navigates the trough and executes on a well-telegraphed restructuring roadmap. We believe investors who are decisive at these levels will be rewarded as sentiment normalizes and the market appropriately revalues IPG's repeatable playbook for margin expansion—our focus remains on execution toward late-'26 inflection as the real catalyst for re-rating.

data.title: PrairieSky Royalty: I'm Picking Up Shares Ahead Of An Oil Rebound
data.description:
Introduction

One of the easiest and safest ways to gain exposure to the oil and gas sector and the prices of fossil fuels is by investing in a royalty company. While they are still exposed to fluctuations in oil and gas prices, they receive a portion of the revenue rather than the operating income and are thus not impacted by operating expenses. The main risk: Operators reduce their output on lands that are subject to royalties payable to the royalty companies. I keep close tabs on two royalty companies in Canada with PrairieSky Royalty Ltd. (TSX:PSK:CA) (OTCPK:PREKF) being the largest one (in terms of market cap and production volumes). As oil and gas prices are under pressure, I wanted to take a look at what we can expect in the current quarter, as PrairieSky’s earnings and FFO will also be under pressure.

A look at the Q1 results and the implication of a lower oil price for 2025

In the first quarter of 2024, PrairieSky Royalty recorded an average daily production volume of just over 25,300 barrels of oil equivalent per day, with oil volumes representing just over 50% of the volume. The average production decreased by approximately 3%. Although the oil volumes increased by approximately 3%, the daily oil-equivalent volumes were negatively impacted by a 10% decrease in natural gas production, and some of that lower production was related to cold weather downtime.

The higher oil production vs. the oil-equivalent production also means the oil sales generated the vast majority of the revenue. As the image below shows, north of C$101M of the C$119.9M in revenue came from the oil sales which means that approximately 54% of the production volumes generated approximately 84% of the total revenue.

After also taking the other revenue into account (mainly related to bonus considerations and lease rental income), the total reported revenue in the first quarter was just over C$128M. As the operating expenses remain very low (as PrairieSky is a royalty company, it doesn’t have to deal with the operational side of running oil and gas wells), the margins are high, and the company reported an EBIT of almost C$80M. As you can see in the image below, approximately 75% of the operating expenses are related to the depletion and depreciation expenses.

The company’s bottom line result indicates a net profit of C$58.4M which works out to C$0.25 per share.

In my previous articles I mainly focused on PrairieSky’s cash flow potential as the company’s royalty strategy means there is a high sunk cost but very little capital required to keep the operations going. Considering the vast majority of the operating expenses are non-cash depletion and depreciation expenses, the net free cash flow should be substantially higher than the reported net income.

Looking at the cash flow statement issued by PrairieSky, we see the company’s funds from operations were a very robust C$85.8M. Divided over the 235.5M shares outstanding, this indicates a net FFO/share of just over C$0.36.

As you can see above the majority of the cash flow was used to acquire almost C$64M in new assets, while the company also paid C$60M in dividends (currently at a rate of C$0.26 per share per quarter) and repurchased C$92M of its own shares (the company also recently renewed its buyback authorization).

The combination of these cash inflows and outflows indeed means the company saw its net debt increase, and the company ended the quarter with a net debt position of C$219M. But given the annualized FFO of north of C$300M, this shouldn’t be an issue, not in the least because we don’t expect the company to spend north of C$60M on asset purchases on a quarterly basis. PrairieSky has a C$325M revolving credit facility (with a permitted upsize to C$600M subject to the lenders’ consent), and there are no liquidity issues. After all, the company generates almost C$1M per day in funds flow (and still C$0.8M at US$60 WTI), so it can reduce the amounts it has drawn on its credit facility pretty fast.

The company’s Q1 revenue and FFO were based on a WTI price of just over US$70/barrel, and it goes without saying the current oil price is a headwind to PrairieSky. The natural gas prices looked a bit stronger a few weeks ago but dropped below C$2/GJ again on the spot markets, so we should anticipate a C$10-15M revenue decrease in the second quarter, and this will likely reduce the FFO/share by C$0.03-0.04 per share on a quarterly basis. This means I expect the current run rate to be around C$1.28-1.30 per share (because the higher net debt also means the net interest expenses will increase as well).

Fortunately, PrairieSky doesn’t have to deal with the operating expenses, but it will still feel the impact of lower sales prices while the operators on PrairieSky royalty lands will likely scale down development activities.

Investment Thesis

Despite that, one of the best ways to gain exposure to oil and gas prices at a below-average risk is having exposure to royalty companies. Sure, the torque will be higher when you buy an oil producer, but owning a royalty company is just safer.

At the current share price, PrairieSky is trading at approximately 17.5 times the annualized FFO of around C$1.30. This still doesn’t make the stock cheap, but quality has its price. I will start to pick up some shares in the near future to gain exposure to the oil price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RCS: Unjustified Premium Valuation And Weak Dividend Coverage
data.description:
Overview

PIMCO Strategic Income Fund (NYSE:RCS) operates as a closed-end fund that aims to provide a diverse range of debt securities that can provide a superior level of income for shareholders. Focusing on debt securities can be an efficient way to offset the market volatility experienced by more traditional equities. This is because debt investments don't require borrowers to rapidly grow earnings. Instead, these types of debt securities only depend on borrowers to make their regularly scheduled debt payments, which is a much safer bet compared to the expectation that equities can continue to grow earnings year after year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RCS's price has increased by 9.4%. When including all of the distributions that were paid out to shareholders over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to 19% over the same time frame. The main appeal of holding a position in RCS is the generous starting dividend yield of 7.2%. In addition, the distributions received from RCS are paid out on a monthly basis, which makes this fund an enticing choice for retired investors. However, I would advise caution in regard to the distribution sustainability at the moment.

RCS has an inception dating back to 1994, which means that we have over three decades' worth of performance data to reference. The fund is actively managed by PIMCO and has a management fee of 0.96%. While the fund may be great for income generation, there are several tradeoffs and risks that investors must consider before initiating a position in RCS. For instance, there should be no expectation for RCS to experience capital appreciation at the moment. Additionally, the price of RCS has displayed sensitivity to the current interest rate environment. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that RCS implements.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's fact sheet, RCS has total managed assets of $301M that are spread across a diverse range of investments. Managers select what they believe to be attractive debt issues across a range of different fixed-income assets. Looking at the specific breakdown below, we can see that Agency MBS (mortgage-backed securities) make up the largest bulk of assets at 54.9%. Agency MBS are securities that are guaranteed by government sponsors and are packages of home loans that have been bundled together. So investors are essentially having their distributions fueled by monthly interest and principal payments.

This is followed by exposure to High Yield Credit and Emerging Market investments, accounting for 10.5% and 7.7% of assets respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up over 73% of total assets, so it's safe to assume that these will be the greatest drivers of RCS's daily price moment and total return potential.

As a way to mitigate the concentration risk to any one specific sector, RCS focuses on a diverse range of industries. For instance, financial-focused investments are the largest focus area, but this only accounts for 6.6% of assets. RCS also has varying exposure to other industries like Technology, Aerospace/Defense, and Media for example. This can prevent the fund from experiencing any cyclical weakness of any one specific industry type.

Lastly, RCS also implements some leverage in an effort to amplify its returns when an opportunity presents itself. The fund essentially takes out debt to purchase securities that may be mispriced or offer the potential for positive returns. The use of leverage currently amounts to 36.4% of assets, which I consider to be quite aggressive in this higher interest rate environment. Since interest rates remain elevated, this means that the cost of debt for RCS is also elevated, and this can make it harder for the leverage to be efficiently utilized. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

Valuation

Since RCS operates as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its net assets. As a result, the price currently trades at a massive premium to NAV of 50.6%. For reference, RCS has traded at an average premium to NAV of 33.45% over the last three-year period. However, the underlying fundamentals and performance don't justify the premium valuation.

In an effort to get more clarity into the large premium, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report from December 2024. Net investment income amounted to $0.24 per share in the latest period, while net realized gains landed at $0.10 per share. Looking back on prior years, it's clear that net investment income levels have seen decreases in recent years. For instance, net investment income was as high as $0.74 per share throughout 2020.

Similarly, net realized gains seem to be wildly inconsistent over the last five years. I imagine that net realized gains are dependent on favorable market conditions that can drive valuations higher. The correlation seems to be that when markets are flat or declining, so is RCS. The underwhelming performance has made it difficult for the fund to experience positive NAV growth as well. For instance, here are the annual NAV amounts over the last few years.

  • 2020: $5.94 per share
  • 2021: $6.55 per share
  • 2022: $4.68 per share
  • 2023: $4.32 per share
  • 2024: $4.39 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $4.42 per share

While the NAV has rebounded from the lows of 2022, the NAV growth and underlying performance aren't that compelling for me. For a fund that prioritizes distributions, I would like to see a more steady level of net investment income over time. The recent decline in net investment income could be a sign that the fund is struggling to operate efficiently in this higher interest rate environment. The only justifying aspect of the portfolio is that the debt securities predominantly have investment-grade credit ratings.

Risk Profile

Looking at the chart below, we can see RCS's price has struggled to recover back to its pre-pandemic level. I suspect that a likely contributor to this is the higher interest rate environment. Higher interest rates directly translate to a higher cost of debt for borrowers. This means that borrowers will see elevated interest expenses, which can put pressure on operating margins. Therefore, higher interest rates generally make these sorts of debt investments less appealing since the risk profile is elevated.

A higher cost of debt may become a problem because it tends to lead to higher defaults from borrowers. As we can see in the chart, RCS's price has remained suppressed since interest rates were rapidly hiked throughout 2022 and 2023. We can also see some optimism around the last set of interest rate cuts that happened around Q4 of 2024, as RCS's price rapidly moved upward before declining again. There is some good news and bad news for RCS.

The good news is that it seems like RCS's portfolio of investments is relatively stable with solid credit ratings. This significantly reduces the risk of RCS experiencing defaults within its portfolio. For reference, debt securities with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade or junk bonds. The breakdown of RCS's credit ratings is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 88.89%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 8.52%
  • Unrated: 2.6%

The bad news is that I believe interest rates are likely to remain elevated for the remainder of 2025. The Fed decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, as they continue to monitor economic data. The full effect of President Trump's tariffs is still to be determined, but I anticipate that rising costs will ultimately slow down economic growth and lead to higher inflationary data. If inflation remains higher than the Fed's targeted 2% level, this may delay interest rate cuts. Although the portfolio's exposure to poor credit ratings is limited, there's still a chance that RCS's price will remain suppressed as the overall debt market deteriorates.

Distribution Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution amount of $0.04 per share, the current dividend yield sits at around 7.2%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.48 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, RCS has an inconsistent history of actually being able to generate enough income to out-earn the distributions it pays out. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($0.46) per share
  • 2021: $1.22 per share
  • 2022: ($1.26) per share
  • 2023: $0.25 per share
  • 2024: $0.68 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.34 per share

As you can see, RCS has only been able to out-earn its distribution in two of the last five full years. Looking at the dividend history below, it makes sense why the dividend payouts have been on the decline over the last decade. Therefore, it becomes very difficult to recommend RCS from an income perspective. The whole reason that investors would want to maintain exposure to these higher-yielding asset classes is for the income they can provide. If that income is under threat of reduction, it defeats the purpose of maintaining exposure in the first place.

To RCS's credit, the fund has still been able to provide positive total returns since its inception three decades ago. Looking at the chart below, we can see that despite RCS's price declining by more than 45.6% since inception, the total return sits around 333% when including all distributions paid out.

Lastly, the distributions received from RCS are typically comprised of a combination of net investment income, net realized gains, or return on capital. The distributions funded by net investment income and net realized gains will have tax consequences that investors must account for. However, return of capital distributions are used when the fund hasn't produced enough income to actually support the distribution. Since the fund's NAV hasn't seen any material growth, I would classify this usage of capital as harmful. So based on your specific tax bracket, amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in RCS would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am rating RCS as a sell due to its high premium valuation, poor distribution history, and underwhelming earnings. While I do see the appeal of a debt-focused fund like RCS, I do not believe that it currently offers an appealing value proposition for long-term investors. RCS offers a diverse range of investments, but the heavy leverage utility limits the appeal of the fund. As we remain in a higher interest rate environment, the valuation of this sort of debt-focused fund continues to be suppressed. The concerns of defaults could be an issue for the entire debt market, despite RCS having a majority of exposure to investment-grade debt.
